metadata.category: Game Theory | Linear Algebra | Bit Manipulation

content.problemStatement:
Johnny is playing Lights Out, an electronic game consisting of an NxN grid of lights. Initially, each light may be on or off. Pressing a light will toggle it and the lights non-diagonally adjacent to it. 


The object of the game is to try and get all the lights turned off. It is not always possible, and what's more, Johnny's game is faulty. Pressing some lights has no effect. (They can still be turned on and off if they are next to working lights.)


Help Johnny write a program that checks whether a puzzle can be solved or not, given the initial description of the grid and the positions of the faulty lights.
constraints: Each grid cell is either '0' or '1'
Faulty positions are distinct and within the grid
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer t (1 ≤ t ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two integers N and K (2 ≤ N ≤ 200, 0 ≤ K ≤ N). The next N lines each contain a string of N characters '0' or '1' describing the initial state of the grid (row‑major order, '1' = on, '0' = off). The following K lines each contain two integers i and j (1‑based) indicating a faulty light at row i, column j. Faulty lights cannot be pressed, but they are still toggled by presses on adjacent cells.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if there exists a sequence of presses that turns all lights off, otherwise output "NO".

solution.approach:
The puzzle can be modelled as a system of linear equations over GF(2). For every cell (i,j) we write an equation that the final state must be 0:
    initial[i][j] + sum of presses that affect (i,j) ≡ 0 (mod 2).
A press toggles the cell itself and its four orthogonal neighbours. Cells that are faulty are not pressable, therefore the corresponding variable is forced to 0 and does not appear as an unknown. All other cells correspond to a binary variable x_{i,j} (press or not). This yields at most N² equations with at most N²‑K unknowns, each equation containing at most five variables, i.e. a very sparse matrix.
We solve the system with Gaussian elimination over GF(2) using bitsets. Each row of the augmented matrix is stored as a dynamic bitset (vector<unsigned long long>) where the last bit holds the right‑hand side. Elimination proceeds column by column, selecting a pivot row that has a 1 in the current column, swapping it to the top, and XOR‑ing it to all rows below that also have a 1 in the column. After the forward pass we perform a backward substitution to check for inconsistency (a row with all zero coefficients but a RHS of 1). If no inconsistency is found the system is solvable and we output "YES"; otherwise "NO".
Because each row contains at most five ones, the number of XOR operations is far smaller than the dense worst‑case bound, and the bitset representation makes each XOR cheap (word‑wise). The algorithm runs comfortably for N up to 200.
Time Complexity: O(V³ / 64) in the dense worst case, where V = N²‑K ≤ 40000, but the actual work is proportional to the number of non‑zero entries (≈5V) and easily fits within limits.
Space Complexity: O(V² / 64) bits for the matrix, i.e. ≤ 40000 × 625 ≈ 25 MiB.
solution.timeComplexity: Approximately O(N⁴ / 64) in the worst dense case; practical runtime is far lower due to sparsity.
